House of
Hatshepsuta fragrance dynasty

Five scents drawn from the ancient routes of frankincense and oud.

The lineThey tried
to erase her.
The story

They tried to erase her name.
Her scent outlasted empires.

She ruled as Pharaoh, Queen and King. She raised the obelisks at Karnak and sent her ships to Punt, returning with living myrrh trees she planted in her own temple gardens. The roots of her myrrh are still in the ground at Deir el-Bahri, three and a half thousand years on. Scent carries her legacy.

The collection

Five pillars hold the temple

Each scent is a portrait, and a column in her temple. A place, a memory, drawn from the ancient routes where spice met stone.

Frankinberri
Frankincense
Wild Strawberry
The oldest resin on earth meets the sweetest fruit of the field. Sacred and playful.
Makeda
Frankincense · Labdanum
Tolu · Bitter Orange
The Queen of Sheba, who carried frankincense across the ancient world to Solomon's court.
Hatshepsut
Agarwood · Myrrh · Opopanax
Rose · Benzoin
The pharaoh herself. Three sacred resins. Makes no announcement. She simply arrives.
Karnak
Frankincense · Black Pepper
Bitter Orange · Cinnamon
The temple she built, raised in scent. Bright resin and slow spice that opens the door to somewhere ancient.
Black Oud
Agarwood · Vetiver · Patchouli
Sandalwood · Pink Pepper
From the black land of Egypt. Deep-rooted and animalic. Meditative, slow, and sure.
Pillar III · The signature

Hatshepsut

Agarwood · Myrrh · Opopanax · Rose · Benzoin

Named for the Foremost of Noble Women. Agarwood gives the soul. Myrrh is dry and ancient, the resin she carried home and planted in her temple. Opopanax and benzoin warm it like honey. It blooms slowly on skin and stays for hours. All depth. No apology.

She makes no announcement. She simply arrives.

An encounter across time

Two queens.
Five hundred years apart.

They never met. Scent is the meeting.

Pillar II · Ancestral continuity

Makeda

Frankincense · Labdanum · Tolu · Bitter Orange

Makeda, the Queen of Sheba, ruled the same sacred roads five centuries after Hatshepsut. She carried frankincense and gold across the desert to Solomon's court. Two queens who would have known each other's caravans by scent alone. In this house, they finally share a temple.

The making

Made by hand, wild harvested.

∴
Wild harvested
Frankincense, myrrh and vetiver, gathered from the earth.
◐
Mother-earth centered
Cruelty-free and pure. Nothing synthetic, only what the earth already made.
◇
Hand-blended
Blended and bottled by hand in small batches, left to deepen before filling.
☥
Crystal, glass & metal
Each scent housed in a vessel made to be kept.
